chunk_summary: This section outlines labeling and traceability protocols for expressed and donor milk in NICUs, emphasizing safety, workflow design, and error prevention strategies, including barcode systems and two-person verification.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of labeling and traceability for expressed and donor milk in NICUs.
Learn best practices for milk storage, inventory rotation, and administration to prevent cross-infant errors.
Identify the role of barcode scanning and two-person verification in reducing wrong-milk events.
Recognize the importance of discarding milk with missing or unreadable labels to ensure safety.
key_facts: Every container must be labeled with infant identifier, mother identifier (for EBM), date/time of expression, and expiry time.
Labels must remain legible under refrigeration or freezing; condensation can cause sticker detachment.
Inventory rotation should follow the FIFO (first-in-first-out) principle within each infant’s supply.
Barcode scanning systems can match bottles to infant wristbands and block expired milk, reducing errors.
In the absence of electronic systems, two-person verification is recommended at preparation and administration.
Donor milk bottles must link to pasteurization batch and culture release records for traceability.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Labels should remain legible when refrigerated or frozen; removable stickers that detach in condensation-prone conditions are a common failure point.
document_key_topics: Labeling and traceability for NICU milk safety
Workflow design for milk handling in NICUs
Donor milk traceability and safety protocols
